docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: GhostSQL is a high-performance, PostgreSQL-compatible SQL database written. It is designed for modern applications that require both scalable relational data and fast vector search for AI/ML workloads.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text: The goal of GhostSQL is to bridge the gap between traditional relational databases and specialized vector stores. By providing a single, unified interface that speaks the PostgreSQL protocol, GhostSQL allows developers to use their favorite tools while gaining native vector acceleration.

docs.title: Key Principles
docs.text:
• Protocol Compatibility: Works with standard psql and PostgreSQL drivers.
  • Vector First: High-performance embeddings storage and HNSW indexing.
  • Relational Integrity: Support for JOINs, Foreign Keys, and ACID properties.
  • Performance: Written in Go with efficient on-disk binary storage.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• RAG Applications: Store documents and their embeddings in the same table.
  • Semantic Search: Build intelligent search into existing relational applications.
  • Edge Computing: Lightweight, single-binary database for distributed environments.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Follow these steps to get GhostSQL up and running on your machine.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Go 1.21 or higher
  • Make (optional, but recommended)

docs.title: Build from Source
docs.text:
Clone the repository and build the binary:

git clone https://github.com/ghosecorp/ghostsql.git
cd ghostsql
make build

docs.title: Running the Server
docs.text:
Start the GhostSQL server on the default port (5433):

./bin/ghostsql-server

docs.title: Connecting with psql
docs.text:
You can use the standard PostgreSQL CLI to connect to GhostSQL:

psql -h localhost -p 5433 -d ghostsql

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text:
Once connected, try creating a table and inserting some data:

CREATE TABLE items (id INT, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO items VALUES (1, 'First Item');
SELECT * FROM items;

docs.title: Onboarding
docs.text:
If you are new to the team, please check the following:

  1. Read the Architecture guide.
  2. Follow the Getting Started instructions.
  3. Run the Test Suite.

docs.title: How to Contribute
docs.text: We welcome contributions from the community!

docs.title: 1. Report Bugs
docs.text: Open an issue on GitHub with a clear description and steps to reproduce.

docs.title: 2. Submit Pull Requests
docs.text:
• Fork the repository.
  • Create a feature branch.
  • Add tests for your changes.
  • Submit a PR with a detailed description.

docs.title: 3. Improve Documentation
docs.text: Documentation fixes and improvements are always appreciated. Edit the files in the docs/ directory and submit a PR.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: GhostSQL is built with a modular architecture to ensure scalability and ease of development.

docs.title: 1. Protocol Layer (internal/protocol)
docs.text: Handles the PostgreSQL wire protocol. It is responsible for parsing incoming packets, managing connections, and formatting response messages (like RowDescription and DataRow).

docs.title: 2. Parser (internal/parser)
docs.text: A custom SQL parser that converts SQL strings into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). It includes: * Lexer: Tokenizes the input string. * Parser: Validates syntax and builds the AST.

docs.title: 3. Executor (internal/executor)
docs.text: The engine that executes the AST. It handles table scans, join logic, filtering (WHERE), sorting (ORDER BY), and vector similarity computations.

docs.title: 4. Storage Engine (internal/storage)
docs.text: Manages on-disk data persistence. * DatabaseInstance: Represents a single database. * Table: Manages rows, schema, and indexes. * Slotted Page Storage: Efficiently manages binary data on disk.

docs.title: Flow of a Query
docs.text:
1. Client Connection: Client connects via psql.
  2. Protocol Handshake: Protocol layer handles authentication and startup.
  3. Request: Client sends a query string.
  4. Parse: Parser converts string to AST.
  5. Execute: Executor processes the AST using the Storage Engine.
  6. Response: Protocol layer sends results back to the client.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: GhostSQL uses a robust testing framework to ensure stability and correctness.

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text: The primary test suite is located in tests/executor_test.go. It performs end-to-end verification by simulating a full database session.

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
To run the integration tests with detailed output:

go test -v ./tests/...

docs.title: What is Tested
docs.text:
• DML & DDL: Table creation, insertion, updating, and deletion.
  • Vector Similarity: Verification of L2 and Cosine distance operators.
  • Relational Logic: Verification of all JOIN types and Foreign Key enforcement.
  • Protocol: Virtualized pg_catalog discovery for psql compatibility.

docs.title: Adding New Tests
docs.text: When adding features, please add a corresponding test case to the tests slice in executor_test.go. Ensure you specify: * name: Descriptive name for the test. * query: The SQL string to execute. * expected: Expected success message (if any). * expectedError: Substring of the error message if the query should fail. * checkRows: Number of rows expected in the result set.

docs.title: Security & Authentication
docs.text: GhostSQL implements a robust authentication and authorization layer compatible with the standard PostgreSQL wire protocol.

docs.title: Authentication Overview
docs.text: GhostSQL uses a Host-Based Authentication (HBA) system, controlled by pg_hba.conf in the data directory.

docs.title: Authentication Modes
docs.text:
• Trust: Permits connections without a password. Default for local loopback (127.0.0.1) connections for non-superusers.
  • Password: Enforces a cleartext password challenge. Required for the administrative account (ghost) and remote connections.

docs.title: Default Credentials
docs.text: Parameter Value Username ghost Password ghost

docs.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
docs.text: GhostSQL uses roles to manage database access and privileges.

docs.title: Role Types
docs.text:
• Login Roles (Users): Created with the LOGIN attribute.
  • Group Roles: Roles that act as groups to manage permissions for multiple users.
  • Superusers: Administrative roles (like ghost) that bypass all permission checks.

docs.title: Table Ownership
docs.text:
When a user creates a table, they become its owner automatically — matching PostgreSQL's pg_aclcheck behavior. The owner always has full access without needing explicit GRANT entries. This means:

  • REVOKE from the owner has no effect (the owner always wins)
  • Ownership is stored in the table's Owner field (analogous to pg_class.relowner)
  • Superusers bypass ownership and ACL checks regardless

docs.title: Example: Managing Roles
docs.text:
-- Create a user with a password
CREATE ROLE alice WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret';

-- Grant connection and creation privileges (PostgreSQL standard order)
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE my_db TO alice;
GRANT CREATE ON DATABASE my_db TO alice;

-- Grant object-level privileges
GRANT SELECT ON TABLE users TO alice;

-- Revoke a privilege
REVOKE UPDATE ON TABLE orders FROM bob;

-- Drop a role
DROP ROLE alice;

docs.title: Supported Privileges
docs.text: Privilege Object Type Description SELECT TABLE Read rows INSERT TABLE Insert rows UPDATE TABLE Update rows DELETE TABLE Delete rows CREATE DATABASE Create tables in a database CONNECT DATABASE Connect to a database ALL PRIVILEGES TABLE, DATABASE All of the above

docs.title: Row-Level Security (RLS)
docs.text: Row-Level Security allows you to define policies that restrict which rows a user can see or modify.

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text:
1. Enable RLS: ALTER TABLE my_table ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
  2. Define Policies: Use CREATE POLICY to define the filtering logic.

docs.title: Example: Private Data
docs.text:
-- Users can only see rows where they are the owner
CREATE POLICY own_records ON documents
FOR SELECT
TO all
USING (owner = current_user());

docs.title: Driver Compatibility
docs.text:
GhostSQL handles the initialization queries that standard PostgreSQL drivers send automatically:

Statement Behavior SET datestyle TO 'ISO' Accepted as no-op BEGIN Accepted (no-op — transactions not yet ACID) COMMIT Accepted as no-op ROLLBACK Accepted as no-op

This ensures compatibility with: - psycopg2 (Python) - pgx (Go) - psql (CLI) - Any standard PostgreSQL wire-protocol client

docs.title: Using psql (Command Line)
docs.text:
# Trusted (local — no password required)
psql -h localhost -p 5433 -d ghostsql

# Authenticated (as ghost)
PGPASSWORD=ghost psql -h localhost -p 5433 -U ghost -d ghostsql

docs.title: Using psycopg2 (Python)
docs.text:
import psycopg2
conn = psycopg2.connect(
    host="localhost", port=5433,
    database="ghostsql", user="ghost", password="ghost"
)
conn.autocommit = True

docs.title: Security Files
docs.text:
• pg_hba.conf: Connection rules (IP, database, user, method).
  • global/pg_authid: Binary role store (analogous to PostgreSQL's pg_authid catalog).

docs.title: Relational Integrity
docs.text: GhostSQL implements standard relational features to ensure data consistency.

docs.title: Primary Keys
docs.text:
Ensure each row is unique:

CREATE TABLE users (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT
);

docs.title: Foreign Keys
docs.text:
Link tables together and enforce referential integrity:

CREATE TABLE departments (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE employees (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    dept_id INT REFERENCES departments(id)
);

docs.title: Joins
docs.text:
GhostSQL supports all major JOIN types:

  • INNER JOIN: Only rows with matches in both tables.
  • LEFT JOIN: All rows from the left table, plus matches from the right.
  • RIGHT JOIN: All rows from the right table, plus matches from the left.
  • FULL OUTER JOIN: All rows from both tables, with NULLs where no match exists.
  • CROSS JOIN: Cartesian product of both tables.

docs.title: Join Example
docs.text:
SELECT e.name, d.name 
FROM employees e 
LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id;

docs.title: SQL Reference
docs.text: GhostSQL supports a broad subset of PostgreSQL-compatible SQL.

docs.title: INSERT
docs.text:
INSERT INTO table_name (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2);
Response tag: INSERT 0 n (PostgreSQL standard, where n is the row count).

docs.title: UPDATE
docs.text: UPDATE table_name SET col1 = val1 WHERE condition;

docs.title: DELETE
docs.text: DELETE FROM table_name WHERE condition;

docs.title: TRUNCATE
docs.text: TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;

docs.title: CREATE TABLE
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    column1 data_type [constraints],
    ...
);
The executing role becomes the owner of the table and has full access automatically.

docs.title: ALTER TABLE
docs.text:
ALTER TABLE table_name ADD COLUMN column_name data_type;
ALTER TABLE table_name ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

docs.title: DROP TABLE
docs.text: DROP TABLE table_name;

docs.title: CREATE ROLE
docs.text:
CREATE ROLE rolename WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'pass';
CREATE ROLE rolename WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'pass' SUPERUSER;

docs.title: DROP ROLE
docs.text: DROP ROLE rolename;

docs.title: GRANT
docs.text:
-- Database-level
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE dbname TO rolename;
GRANT CREATE ON DATABASE dbname TO rolename;

-- Table-level
GRANT SELECT ON TABLE tablename TO rolename;
GRANT INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON TABLE tablename TO rolename;
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON TABLE tablename TO rolename;

docs.title: REVOKE
docs.text: REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE tablename FROM rolename;

docs.title: CREATE POLICY (Row-Level Security)
docs.text:
CREATE POLICY policy_name ON table_name
FOR SELECT
TO all
USING (owner_column = current_user());

docs.title: Transaction Control
docs.text:
GhostSQL accepts but does not yet fully implement ACID transactions. These statements are parsed and accepted as no-ops:

BEGIN;
COMMIT;
ROLLBACK;

docs.title: Session Variables
docs.text: SET datestyle TO 'ISO';   -- accepted as no-op (driver compatibility)

docs.title: SHOW TABLES
docs.text: List all tables in the current database.

docs.title: SHOW COLUMNS
docs.text: SHOW COLUMNS FROM table_name;

docs.title: COMMENT ON
docs.text: COMMENT ON TABLE table_name IS 'description';

docs.title: System Catalog (pg_catalog)
docs.text:
SELECT relname FROM pg_catalog.pg_class WHERE relkind = 'r';
SELECT nspname FROM pg_catalog.pg_namespace;

docs.title: Vector Search
docs.text: GhostSQL provides first-class support for vector embeddings and similarity search.

docs.title: Vector Type
docs.text:
You can define columns with the VECTOR(n) type, where n is the number of dimensions.

CREATE TABLE embeddings (
    id INT,
    vec VECTOR(3)
);

docs.title: Similarity Operators
docs.text:
GhostSQL supports standard PostgreSQL similarity operators:

  • <->: L2 (Euclidean) distance.
  • <=>: Cosine distance.

docs.title: Example Query
docs.text:
-- Find the closest row using L2 distance
SELECT * FROM embeddings 
ORDER BY vec <-> '[0.1, 0.2, 0.3]' 
LIMIT 1;

docs.title: Vector Functions
docs.text:
For compatibility, GhostSQL also supports functional syntax:

  • L2_DISTANCE(v1, v2)
  • COSINE_DISTANCE(v1, v2)
SELECT id FROM embeddings 
ORDER BY COSINE_DISTANCE(vec, '[0.9, 0.1, 0.0]') 
LIMIT 5;

docs.title: Indexing (HNSW)
docs.text:
For large datasets, you can create an HNSW index to speed up nearest neighbor searches:

CREATE INDEX my_idx ON embeddings USING HNSW (vec) WITH (m=16, ef_construction=200);
